howItWorks.intro: Diluting spirits with water lowers the alcohol by volume (ABV) percentage. The key formula comes from Pearson's square, which calculates how much water (0% ABV) to add to reach a target concentration.
rules: Formula: Water to add = Current Volume × (Current ABV / Target ABV − 1)
Proof vs ABV: US Proof = ABV × 2. So 80 proof = 40% ABV.
This formula assumes ideal mixing. In practice, alcohol + water has slight volume contraction (~3-5%), so the actual final volume is slightly less than calculated.
howItWorks.outro: Use distilled or filtered water for best results. Add water slowly and stir. For spirits, let the diluted product rest for at least 24-48 hours before tasting, as the flavor integrates over time. Temperature affects the reading — standard is 60°F (15.6°C).

faq.question: What is Pearson's square?
faq.answer: Pearson's square is a simple method for calculating mixing ratios to achieve a target concentration. For alcohol dilution, it compares the current ABV and target ABV to determine the ratio of spirit to water needed. The formula simplifies to: water = volume × (current_ABV / target_ABV − 1).

faq.question: Does mixing alcohol and water change the total volume?
faq.answer: Yes, slightly. When ethanol and water mix, the total volume contracts by about 3-5% compared to the sum of the individual volumes. This is due to hydrogen bonding between molecules. For home use, this difference is negligible. Professional distillers account for it.

faq.question: Why do distillers proof down spirits?
faq.answer: Spirits come off the still at very high proof (often 130-160+ proof). Most spirits are bottled at 80-100 proof. Proofing down with water makes the spirit drinkable, brings out flavor complexity, and meets legal bottling requirements.

faq.question: Can I use tap water to dilute spirits?
faq.answer: Use distilled or carbon-filtered water. Tap water may contain chlorine, minerals, or off-flavors that can affect the taste of your spirits. Reverse-osmosis water also works well. Avoid mineral water unless you specifically want those mineral characteristics.

faq.question: How do I measure ABV at home?
faq.answer: Use an alcoholmeter (also called a proof hydrometer). Float it in a sample of your spirit at 60°F (15.6°C) and read the scale where it meets the liquid surface. Digital refractometers also work. Sugar content affects readings, so use a hydrometer for sugared spirits.

faq.question: Can I use this for diluting wine must or mead?
faq.answer: Yes, the same formula applies. When diluting wine must or mead, you are lowering the potential alcohol by diluting the sugar concentration. Be aware that dilution also affects flavor intensity, acidity, and body.

sections.title: What Is the Alcohol Dilution Calculator?
sections.body: The Alcohol Dilution Calculator is a free online tool designed for home cooks and chefs who need quick, accurate calculations in the cooking and food preparation space. By entering your current abv %, target abv %, current volume, you get instant results including water to add, final volume. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting water to add right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In cooking and food preparation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by home cooks and chefs worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to perfect your recipes with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your current abv % and need to find the right water to add. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.
